section.title: The Day of the Lord
section.sectionRationale: Peter turns from the false teachers to the doubt they spread: that Christ will never return. He answers the scoffers of the last days by appealing to God's word, which once judged the world by water and now reserves it for fire. The seeming delay is not slowness but patience, for the Lord measures time differently and is not willing that any should perish. When the Day comes it will come like a thief, dissolving the heavens and the elements, and God's people wait for a new heaven and a new earth where righteousness dwells.
section.thesis: The apparent delay of the Day of the Lord is not God's failure to keep His promise but His patience toward sinners; the same word that made and once flooded the world holds it now for fire, so that when the Day comes suddenly and the present order is dissolved, believers who look for the new heaven and new earth should live in holiness and godliness.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the scoffers of the last days, verses 1 to 4; God's word over past and coming judgment, verses 5 to 7; the Lord not slow but patient, verses 8 to 9; and the Day of the Lord and the new creation, verses 10 to 13.

units.label: Scoffers in the last days
units.note: where is the promise of His coming

verses.text: Beloved, this is now my second letter to you. Both of them are reminders to stir you to wholesome thinking
by recalling what was foretold by the holy prophets and commanded by our Lord and Savior through your apostles.
Most importantly, you must understand that in the last days {{empaiktai|scoffers}} will come, scoffing and following their own evil desires.
“Where is the promise of {{parousia|His coming}}?” they will ask. “Ever since our fathers fell asleep, everything continues as it has from the beginning of creation.”

units.label: The word that judges
units.note: once by water, now reserved for fire

verses.text: But they deliberately overlook the fact that long ago by God’s word the heavens existed and the earth was formed out of water and by water,
through which the world of that time perished in the flood.
And by that same word, the present heavens and earth are reserved for fire, being kept for the day of judgment and destruction of ungodly men.

units.label: Not slow, but patient
units.note: not willing that any should perish

verses.text: Beloved, do not let this one thing escape your notice: With the Lord a day is like {{chilia|a thousand years}}, and a thousand years are like a day.
The Lord is not slow in keeping His promise as some understand slowness, but is {{makrothumei|patient}} with you, not wanting anyone to perish but everyone to come to {{metanoia|repentance}}.

units.label: The Day and the new creation
units.note: like a thief, then righteousness dwells

verses.text: But the Day of the Lord will come like a {{kleptes|thief}}. The heavens will disappear with a roar, the {{stoicheia|elements}} will be destroyed by fire, and the earth and its works will be laid bare.
Since everything will be destroyed in this way, what kind of people ought you to be? You ought to conduct yourselves in holiness and godliness
as you anticipate and hasten the coming of the day of God, when the heavens will be destroyed by fire and the elements will melt in the heat.
But in keeping with God’s promise, we are looking forward to a {{kainos|new}} heaven and a new earth, where {{dikaiosyne|righteousness}} dwells.

empaiktai.gloss: Mockers, scoffers, those who make sport of what is sacred. In the last days such men will jeer at the promise of Christ's return, and their scoffing is driven not by reason but by their own evil desires. They mock the coming judgment because they do not want it to be true.

parousia.gloss: Presence, arrival, coming, the standard word for the royal visit of a king. Used of the return of Christ in glory, His parousia is the promise the scoffers deny. The whole hope of the church rests on the certainty of His coming.

chilia.gloss: A thousand years. Echoing Psalm 90:4, Peter reminds believers that God is not bound by time as we are: a day and a thousand years are alike to Him. What looks like delay to impatient men is nothing to the eternal God who keeps His own perfect timing.

makrothumei.gloss: To be long-suffering, patient, slow to anger. The seeming delay of the Day is in fact God's patience, holding back judgment to give room for repentance. His slowness, so called, is mercy: He is not willing that any should perish.

metanoia.gloss: A change of mind and heart, repentance, a turning from sin to God. God's patience has a purpose: that sinners come to repentance. The interval before the Day is the space of grace in which the gospel calls men to turn and live.

kleptes.gloss: A thief. The Day of the Lord will come like a thief, unannounced and unexpected, catching the careless unprepared. The image, drawn from Jesus' own teaching, presses the call to watchfulness: no one knows the hour, so all must be ready.

stoicheia.gloss: The elements, the basic constituents of the physical world. On the Day these will be destroyed, or dissolved, by fire. Peter pictures the undoing of the present created order, the whole fabric of the world laid bare before its Maker and Judge.

kainos.gloss: New in kind and quality, not merely new in time. The promised new heaven and new earth are not just a fresh copy of the old but a renewed creation, cleansed and glorious, where righteousness is at home. This is the hope that shapes how believers live now.

dikaiosyne.gloss: Righteousness, conformity to what is right, the settled character of God Himself. In the new creation righteousness dwells, is at home, permanently. The world to come is not merely free of sin but full of the righteousness the scoffers now despise.

basic.context: Peter writes to stir wholesome thinking against a rising doubt. In the last days mockers arise, ruled by their own desires, sneering that Christ has not returned and that life simply goes on as always. Peter answers on three fronts. First, the scoffers wilfully forget history: the same word of God that made the world once destroyed it by the flood, and that word now holds the present world for fire on the day of judgment. Second, the delay is not slowness but patience; God is not bound by time, and He withholds judgment so that sinners may repent. Third, the Day will come suddenly, like a thief, dissolving the heavens and the elements in fire. Because all this is coming, believers should live holy and godly lives, waiting for the new heaven and new earth where righteousness dwells.
basic.hermeneutics: This passage is prophetic and eschatological, teaching about the return of Christ and the end of the age. Read it in light of Jesus' own words about His coming as a thief and no one knowing the day or hour. Two matters draw special care. First, the so-called delay of the Day: what looks to the scoffers like a broken promise is, in Peter's answer, the outworking of God's patience. Second, the language of fire, dissolving elements, and the earth laid bare: interpreters differ on whether this describes the annihilation of the present cosmos or its purifying renewal into the new creation of verse 13.
basic.originalLanguages: In the last days come empaiktai, scoffers, who deny the parousia, the coming of Christ. Peter answers that with the Lord a day is like chilia ete, a thousand years; He is not slow but makrothumei, patient, willing all to come to metanoia, repentance. Yet the Day will come like a kleptes, a thief, when the stoicheia, the elements, are dissolved by fire, and believers await a kainos, a qualitatively new, heaven and earth where dikaiosyne, righteousness, dwells.
basic.typology: The flood of Noah's day is a type of the coming judgment: as the ancient world perished by water at God's word, so the present world is reserved for fire by that same word. The pattern of a world judged and a remnant preserved points forward to the final Day and to the new creation that follows, cleansed and secure.
basic.christ: The whole passage hangs on the certainty of Christ's coming. The promise the scoffers deny is the promise of His parousia, His royal return in glory. He is the Lord whose Day this is, the Judge before whom the elements dissolve, and the Savior whose patience now holds open the door of repentance. The new heaven and new earth are His inheritance shared with His people, and it is knowing Him that makes the believer ready for the Day that comes like a thief.
basic.god: God is Creator and Judge, whose word made the heavens and the earth, once destroyed the world by flood, and now keeps it for fire. He is sovereign over time, to whom a thousand years are as a day, so that no promise of His can fail through delay. And He is patient and merciful, not willing that any should perish, holding back the Day so that sinners may repent. His justice and His mercy meet in the interval before the end.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 90:4 stands behind verse 8, a thousand years as a day in God's sight. Matthew 24:36-44 gives Jesus' teaching on the unknown day and the thief. Genesis 6 to 8 tells of the flood, the pattern of verses 5 to 7. And Isaiah 65:17 and 66:22 promise the new heavens and new earth fulfilled in Revelation 21:1.
basic.oneStory: From creation to flood to the coming fire, God's word governs the whole story of the world, and it moves toward a settled end: a new heaven and new earth where righteousness is at home. The scoffers read history as an endless sameness, but Scripture reads it as a road to the Day of the Lord. Between the promise and its fulfilment stands God's patience, the space of grace in which the gospel of the returning Christ calls sinners to repent before that Day arrives.

context.literary: The chapter opens a new and final section, marked by the affectionate beloved and the appeal to remember. It moves by a clear logic: the scoffers' claim, verses 3 to 4; the answer from God's word and the flood, verses 5 to 7; the answer from God's patience, verses 8 to 9; and the certainty of the Day with its ethical demand, verses 10 to 13. The argument builds from denial to judgment to hope, ending on the new creation.
context.historical: Peter writes to churches unsettled by teachers who denied a future judgment and reckoning, likely mocking the church's hope as the first generation died and Christ had not yet returned. The claim that everything continues unchanged reflects a view of the world as a closed, uniform system. Peter counters with the biblical record of creation and flood, decisive interruptions by the God who acts.
context.canonical: The passage joins the Bible's teaching on the Day of the Lord, drawing prophetic imagery of fire and cosmic upheaval into the Christian hope of Christ's return. It stands with Jesus' Olivet teaching and with Paul's word that the Day comes like a thief in the night. Its promise of a new heaven and new earth reaches back to Isaiah and forward to Revelation 21, framing the whole canon's end.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To fortify believers against scoffers who deny Christ's return, by grounding the certainty of the coming Day in God's word and past acts of judgment, reframing the delay as patience aimed at repentance, and pressing the sure end of the present order into a call to holy, godly, expectant living.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage both describes what is coming, the Day, the fire, the new creation, and prescribes how to live in light of it: holiness, godliness, watchfulness, and eager hope. The scoffers' words are reported to be refuted, not adopted. The abiding command is to remember the promise, to reckon rightly with God's patience, and to be found ready.

debates.question: Why has the Day of the Lord seemingly been delayed, verses 3 to 9?

views.label: There is no true delay; the seeming delay is divine patience
views.case: Peter's own answer is that the delay is not slowness at all. God is not bound by time, so that to Him a day and a thousand years are alike, and no promise fails through the passing of years. What looks like lateness is His long-suffering mercy, holding back judgment so that sinners may come to repentance. The interval is grace, not failure, and it will end at the appointed hour.

views.label: The scoffers are right that the promise has failed
views.case: The scoffers argue from uniformity: since the fathers died, everything continues as from the beginning, so the promise of His coming is empty. Peter names this view precisely to overturn it. They deliberately overlook creation and the flood, God's decisive interruptions of the world, and so their argument from mere sameness collapses. Their real problem is moral, not chronological: they follow their own evil desires.

debates.bearing: Peter settles the question decisively: the Day is certain and the delay is mercy. This guards believers against both despair and complacency. It answers the mockery not by setting dates but by preaching God's patience, which should lead not to presumption but to repentance and holy expectancy, since the Day will come suddenly, like a thief.

debates.question: Does the fire annihilate the present cosmos or purify and renew it, verses 10 to 13?

views.label: The present order is destroyed and replaced
views.case: The heavens pass away with a roar, the elements are dissolved by fire, and the earth is laid bare. Some read this as the annihilation of the present creation, wholly consumed and then replaced by a new heaven and new earth made afresh. The stress falls on the total undoing of the world that now is, so that the new creation is a fresh act of God.

views.label: The present order is purged and renewed
views.case: Others read the fire as purifying rather than annihilating, echoing the flood which destroyed the world's corruption yet left the earth to continue. The earth being laid bare, or found, suggests exposure to judgment rather than obliteration, and the new heaven and new earth are the old creation liberated and renewed, in line with Romans 8 and Revelation 21. The continuity of a redeemed cosmos is stressed.

debates.bearing: Godly interpreters differ, partly because of a textual variant in verse 10, whether the earth is laid bare, found, or burned up. Either way Peter's pastoral point stands: the present order is not ultimate, judgment by fire is certain, and righteousness will dwell in what God finally makes. The right response is holiness and hope, not speculation about the mechanics of the end.

study.originalLanguages: Peter's answer to doubt is dense with meaning. The empaiktai, scoffers, mock the parousia, the royal coming of Christ, reasoning from the world's sameness. But God is not measured by our clocks: to Him a day is as chilia ete, a thousand years. He is not slow but makrothumei, long-suffering, and His patience has an aim, that all come to metanoia, a true turning of the heart. Yet the Day will break in like a kleptes, a thief, when the stoicheia, the elemental substances of the world, are dissolved by fire and the earth laid bare before its Judge. Beyond the fire lies a kainos, a qualitatively new, heaven and earth, where dikaiosyne, righteousness, is permanently at home. The vocabulary moves from mockery to mercy to the sudden Day to the settled righteousness of the world to come.

typology.fulfillment: The ancient world judged by water at God's word is the pattern of the world reserved for fire, both showing that God interrupts history in decisive judgment, Genesis 6 to 8.

typology.type: The preserved remnant of the flood
typology.fulfillment: As Noah's household was carried safely through judgment, so God's people are kept for the new creation, a pledge that judgment and salvation come together on the Day.

typology.type: The day of the Lord in the prophets
typology.fulfillment: The prophetic Day of fire and cosmic shaking is fulfilled in the return of Christ and the dissolving of the present order, Joel 2 and Isaiah 34.

study.christ: Everything here turns on Christ. The promise the scoffers deny is the promise of His coming, His parousia in glory as King and Judge. He is the Lord whose Day this is, before whom the heavens flee and the elements melt, and He is also the patient Savior whose long-suffering now holds open the door of repentance so that none need perish. The new heaven and new earth are the world He will make right, where His righteousness dwells at last. To know Him is to be ready for the Day that comes like a thief, and to love His appearing rather than to dread it.
study.god: God is revealed as Creator, Sustainer, and Judge. By His word the heavens and earth came to be, by His word the ancient world was drowned, and by that same word the present world is held for fire. He stands above time, so that no promise of His can grow stale, and He is patient and merciful, not willing that any should perish but that all come to repentance. His justice guarantees the Day; His mercy explains the wait. Both together call His people to holiness and hope.

crossReferences.note: A thousand years in Your sight are like a day just gone by, the text behind verse 8 and God's relation to time.
No one knows the day or hour, and the Son of Man comes like a thief, the source of verse 10's image.
The flood by which the world of that time perished, the historical pattern of verses 5 to 7.
The promise of a new heaven and new earth where righteousness dwells, fulfilled in the world to come, verse 13.

study.oneStory: Scripture tells one story of a world made, judged, and remade by the word of God. Creation, flood, and the coming fire are chapters of a single account moving toward the new heaven and new earth where righteousness dwells. The scoffers see only endless sameness, but the God who once interrupted the world by water will interrupt it again by fire, at the appointed hour. Between promise and fulfilment stands His patience, the age of grace in which the returning Christ is preached, so that sinners may repent and be found ready for the Day.

sermon.title: The Day of the Lord
sermon.why: From Begg's series A Study in 2 Peter, this message expounds 2 Peter 3:1-13 directly: the scoffers of the last days, the answer from God's word and the flood, the Lord's patience toward repentance, and the sudden Day of the Lord with the promise of the new creation. A clear, orthodox handling of this movement.

quiz.q: On what basis do the scoffers deny the promise of Christ's coming in verses 3 to 4?

options.text: They claim everything continues unchanged as it has from the beginning of creation.
options.note: Verse 4: their argument is from the apparent uniformity of the world since the fathers died.

options.text: They have carefully studied the prophets and found the promise false.
options.note: Peter says they deliberately overlook God's word and are driven by their own desires, not study.

options.text: They mock the promise while following their own evil desires.
options.note: True to verse 3, though the stated argument is the world's sameness in verse 4.

options.text: They believe Christ already returned in secret.
options.note: The scoffers deny His coming altogether; they do not claim a secret return.

quiz.q: How does Peter answer the charge that God is slow to keep His promise, verses 8 to 9?

options.text: The delay is God's patience, not wanting anyone to perish but all to come to repentance.
options.note: Verse 9: what looks like slowness is long-suffering mercy aimed at repentance.

options.text: God has simply forgotten the promise He made.
options.note: The opposite: no promise of God can fail, for He is not bound by time.

options.text: With the Lord a day is like a thousand years, so He is not bound by our sense of time.
options.note: True to verse 8, though the heart of the answer is His patience toward repentance.

options.text: The promise applies to a later generation, not to Peter's readers.
options.note: Peter says the Lord is patient with you; the delay is mercy now, not a deferral to others.

quiz.q: What does Peter recall about God's word to refute the scoffers in verses 5 to 7?

options.text: By God's word the world was made and once destroyed by flood, and that same word now reserves it for fire.
options.note: Verses 5 to 7: God's word both created and judged the world, and holds it for the coming Day.

options.text: God's word promises the world will never change again.
options.note: The reverse: the world faces a decisive judgment by fire.

options.text: The present heavens and earth are reserved for the day of judgment.
options.note: True to verse 7, though Peter's fuller point spans creation, flood, and coming fire.

options.text: God's word applies only to the spiritual realm, not the physical world.
options.note: Peter's argument is precisely about the physical world made, flooded, and reserved for fire.

quiz.q: How does Peter describe the manner of the Day of the Lord's arrival in verse 10?

options.text: It will come suddenly and unexpectedly, like a thief.
options.note: Verse 10: the image of the thief presses watchfulness, echoing Jesus' own teaching.

options.text: It will be announced years in advance by clear signs everyone will heed.
options.note: The point is suddenness; it comes like a thief, catching the careless unprepared.

options.text: It will bring the dissolving of the heavens and the elements by fire.
options.note: True to verse 10, though the question asks about the suddenness of its arrival.

options.text: It will come only after the whole world has been converted.
options.note: Peter ties the Day to God's patience for repentance, not to universal conversion first.

quiz.q: In light of the coming Day, how should believers live, verses 11 to 13?

options.text: In holiness and godliness, looking forward to the new heaven and new earth where righteousness dwells.
options.note: Verses 11 to 13: the certain end of the present order calls for holy, expectant living.

options.text: In fear and withdrawal, since nothing they do matters.
options.note: Peter calls for active holiness and hope, not fearful passivity.

options.text: By anticipating and hastening the coming of the day of God.
options.note: True to verse 12, though the fuller command includes holiness and the hope of the new creation.

options.text: By calculating the date of Christ's return from the signs.
options.note: The Day comes like a thief; the call is readiness, not date-setting.

questions: The scoffers argued that because life carries on unchanged, God will not act. Where are you tempted to read the world's ordinary sameness as evidence that God is distant or silent?
Peter reframes the delay of the Day as God's patience toward repentance. How does seeing the interval as mercy rather than absence change the way you pray for those who do not yet believe?
The Day will come like a thief. What would it look like for you to be genuinely ready today rather than assuming there is always more time?
Peter says the certainty of the coming Day should make us holy and godly. Which area of your life would most change if you truly lived as though this world is not the last word?
Believers look forward to a new heaven and new earth where righteousness dwells. How does that final hope steady you when the present world seems given over to injustice and decay?

translationNotes.note: With the Lord a day is like a thousand years echoes Psalm 90:4; the point is God's transcendence over time, not a formula for calculating the end.
Patient renders makrothymeo, long-suffering; not wanting anyone to perish expresses God's gracious disposition toward repentance.
The elements be destroyed renders a verb meaning dissolved, also in verses 11 and 12; a textual variant reads the earth will be laid bare, or found, while some manuscripts read burned up.
New in a new heaven and a new earth renders kainos, new in kind, a renewed and righteous creation, echoing Isaiah 65:17 and 66:22.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 2 Peter 3:1-13. All verses confirmed against the API. No dashes appear in this movement's text.
